skill.name: pathml
skill.description: Guides computational pathology workflows with PathML, including whole-slide image loading, preprocessing, graph construction, model training, and multiplex imaging analysis.
skill.summary: Plan PathML workflows for whole-slide pathology imaging and spatial analysis.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high signals, but most are false positives from fenced Markdown examples and normal scientific Python terms. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The remaining risk is medium because some examples could teach unsafe local execution patterns if copied without safeguards.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe PyTorch Model Loading Guidance
medium_findings.description: The documentation loads a model with torch.load. This is legitimate for trusted local models, but it can execute code if users load an untrusted pickle-backed model file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly demonstrates torch.load on a model path. The context is local feature extraction, so this is unsafe guidance rather than malicious skill behavior.

medium_findings.title: Unquoted Shell Variable in Generated Job Script
medium_findings.description: The SLURM example passes $SLIDE_PATH without quotes. If copied into a workflow with untrusted file names, shell metacharacters or spaces could change command behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippet is clear and the variable is unquoted. The path comes from local slide lists, so exploitation depends on attacker-controlled file names.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Findings Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The eval findings refer to model.eval calls in PyTorch examples, not Python eval on user input. Dynamic import hits are import statements inside fenced examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines are fenced Markdown examples and standard PathML or PyTorch usage. No user-controlled eval call was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Fence Artifacts
low_findings.description: Many external command hits point to code fence delimiters and example snippets. They do not run automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are visibly documentation snippets. They require deliberate user execution and are aligned with the PathML domain.

low_findings.title: Promotional External Platform Suggestion
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is promotional and should be transparent, but it does not override security review or request secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and points to an external service. It does not include prompt-injection language or data exfiltration instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Unsafe Local Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains examples for shell job submission and model loading. These are normal for scientific workflows, but users should add quoting and only load trusted model files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both examples are visible documentation, not hidden execution logic. The risk is practical misuse when copied into real pipelines.

content.value_statement: Whole-slide pathology workflows require many format, preprocessing, and model decisions. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan PathML workflows with focused reference guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains PathML workflows for loading whole-slide images and multiparametric imaging data.
Guides preprocessing pipeline design for tissue detection, stain normalization, and artifact handling.
Covers graph construction for cell, tissue, and spatial omics analysis.
Summarizes machine learning workflows for HoVer-Net, HACTNet, evaluation, and ONNX inference.
Describes HDF5 storage, tile management, batch processing, and dataset organization.
limitations: Does not install PathML or verify local dependency compatibility.
Does not diagnose pathology images without user-provided data and tool access.
Does not replace clinical review by qualified pathology professionals.
Some examples require large compute resources and careful local security controls.

use_cases.title: Build a Whole-Slide Preprocessing Plan
use_cases.description: Design a repeatable H&E preprocessing workflow with tissue detection, stain normalization, tiling, and HDF5 storage.

use_cases.title: Prepare Multiplex Imaging Analysis
use_cases.description: Plan CODEX or Vectra workflows for channel handling, cell segmentation, marker quantification, and AnnData export.

use_cases.title: Review Pathology ML Training Steps
use_cases.description: Map dataset preparation, model selection, validation metrics, and inference deployment for nucleus analysis tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Loading Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: I have whole-slide images in [format]. Explain which PathML loading approach to use and what metadata to inspect first.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Preprocessing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PathML preprocessing plan for H&E slides that includes quality control, tissue detection, stain normalization, and tiling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multiplex Quantification
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a CODEX analysis in PathML from channel collapse through segmentation, marker quantification, and AnnData export.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Training Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned HoVer-Net training workflow for data splits, preprocessing consistency, metrics, model loading safety, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: User asks how to process a folder of SVS slides for tissue detection and storage.
output: Recommended workflow stages: load slides, inspect pyramid levels, generate tiles, run tissue detection, save HDF5 outputs.
Safety notes: validate paths, process a small subset first, and avoid overwriting raw data.

output_examples.input: User asks how to analyze CODEX data with marker quantification.
output: Suggested approach: load CODEX data, collapse acquisition cycles, segment cells, quantify markers, and export cell-level tables.
Validation notes: confirm channel names, inspect segmentation masks, and verify spatial coordinates before downstream analysis.

output_examples.input: User asks how to prepare a nucleus segmentation model workflow.
output: Plan includes dataset preparation, augmentation choices, HoVer-Net configuration, validation metrics, and inference packaging.
Risk note: load only trusted model files and document preprocessing parameters used during training.

best_practices: Start with a small representative slide set before scaling to a full cohort.
Keep raw slides separate from processed HDF5 files and record preprocessing parameters.
Quote shell variables and load only trusted model files in copied workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run batch jobs before validating tile size, pyramid level, and tissue masks.
Do not mix slides from different scanners without checking magnification and staining differences.
Do not treat model predictions as clinical findings without expert review.

faq.question: What is PathML used for?
faq.answer: PathML supports computational pathology workflows for whole-slide imaging, preprocessing, graph analysis, machine learning, and multiplex imaging.

faq.question: Can this skill read my slide files directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance unless the host environment also gives the assistant file and tool access.

faq.question: Which slide formats are covered?
faq.answer: The references discuss formats such as SVS, NDPI, SCN, DICOM, OME-TIFF, CODEX, Vectra, and MERFISH.

faq.question: Does it support machine learning workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers HoVer-Net, HACTNet, PyTorch data loading, evaluation metrics, and ONNX inference planning.

faq.question: Is it safe to copy every example directly?
faq.answer: No. Review paths, shell quoting, model sources, and local data permissions before running examples.

faq.question: Can it replace a pathologist?
faq.answer: No. It helps with technical workflow planning and does not provide clinical diagnosis.
